text: In this video, we present a case of laparoscopic total pelvic accent duration for a locally advanced CA rectum.
A nineteen years old gentleman presented to us with bleeding per rectum and was diagnosed with non-metastatic low rectal adenocarcinoma.
MRI scan revealed low rectal growth infiltrating the prostate and the seminal vesicles.

He received long-course concurrent chemoradiotherapy and was planned for total pelvic exenteration as the post CT-RT MRI showed partial response but the disease was still infiltrating the prostate and seminal vesicles.
The surgical team utilizes a five-port laparoscopic approach, with the ports positioned in a standard configuration for the procedure.
The surgeon begins dissection to the left of the duodenal jejunal flexure, carefully separating tissue in this area.
The surgeon lifts the inferior mesenteric vein and performs dissection in the avascular plane posterior to the vein, carefully separating the tissue layers.
The surgeon mobilizes the redundant sigmoid colon laterally, incising the lateral peritoneal fold and dissecting the sigmoid colon away from the ureter and gonadal vessels.
The surgeon refrains from fully mobilizing the splenic flexure during the abdominoperineal resection (APR) procedure.
The surgeon retracts the sigmoid colon anteriorly and incises the medial peritoneal fold just medial to the right ureter, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection in the avascular embryonic plane, continuing the separation of tissue layers without vascular interference.
The surgeon carefully dissects in the avascular plane, taking special care to preserve the important autonomic nerves during the procedure.
The surgeon continues the lateral dissection, carefully safeguarding the ureter and gonadal vessels, ensuring these structures remain intact during the procedure.
Following lymph nodal clearance, the surgeon clips and divides the inferior mesenteric artery at its root, ensuring proper vascular control.
The surgeon performs posterior dissection in the extra mesorectal avascular plane, carefully separating tissue layers without disturbing vascular structures.
The surgeon uses adequate traction to facilitate dissection in the avascular embryonic plane, ensuring precise separation of tissue layers.
The surgeon extends the lateral peritoneal cut in the pelvis, exposing the left iliac vessels while continuing dissection up to the pelvic floor.
The surgeon exposes the obliterated umbilical artery, which is visibly arising from the internal iliac artery within the pelvic region.
The surgeon ligates and divides the left obliterated umbilical artery, which is visible arising from the internal iliac artery.
The surgeon dissects the internal iliac artery distally, identifying and ligating the middle rectal artery and inferior vesicle artery before dividing them.
The surgeon extends the peritoneal fold cut anteriorly to expose the left vas, which is then clipped and divided.

The surgeon extends the right peritoneal cut inferiorly to expose the right iliac vessels. The procedure is repeated on the right side, involving the division of the superior vesicle artery, obliterated umbilical artery, middle rectal artery, inferior vesicle artery, and the vas deferens.
The surgeon joins the bilateral peritoneal cuts anteriorly and initiates dissection in the prevesical space, revealing the anatomical structures in this area.
The surgeon divides the urachus and continues the dissection inferiorly, separating the bladder from the anterior abdominal wall up to the pubic arch.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to divide the lateral bladder attachments, continuing the dissection inferiorly to fully expose the levators and the endopelvic fascia on both sides.
The surgeon incises the endopelvic fascia on both sides of the dorsal venous complex, revealing the underlying prostatic capsule.
The surgeon uses a harmonic energy source to clip, seal, and divide the completely exposed dorsal venous complex.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to divide the periurethral tissue, followed by transection of the urethra.
The surgeon clips and divides the right ureter at the uretero-vesical junction, releasing it from its retroperitoneal attachments.
The surgeon divides the left ureter at the uretero-vesical junction and releases it from its retroperitoneal attachments.
The surgeon frees the left ureter from surrounding tissues, elevating it as high as possible to facilitate future uretero-conduit anastomosis.

The surgeon divides the mesocolon at a predetermined level and transects the distal descending colon using a linear endoscopic stapler, marking the completion of the laparoscopic phase of the surgery.
The surgeon performs perineal dissection, delivering the surgical specimen through the perineal approach.
The surgeon performs a six-centimeter lower midline laparotomy, creating an ileal conduit extracorporeally.
The surgeon fashions both the permanent colostomy and the conduit stoma, followed by closing the midline incision.
The patient is positioned prone, and the plastic surgery team performs pelvic floor repair using bilateral V-Y advancement flaps.
The procedure was uneventful and the patient was discharged on post-operative day nine without any complications.

Histopathology showed residual adenocarcinoma with stage T-to-N-zero, a free circumferential resection margin, and acellular mucin in the prostate and seminal vesicles suggestive of good response to CT-RT.
